note: keystone-recovered 2026-07-11 from workflow journal wf_393dc2ed-49c: gap.json draw field was a stub on disk; the assay received THIS full object in its prompt (defect #7 residue — transcript fixed by the prompt hardening, draw slipped once).
transcript:
GAP SEAT (hold-apart, xor) — Fiat-Shamir witness derivation for r4-compose-line-edit.

=== STEP 1: NUMBERED FACT LIST (original artifact/GUIDE.md body, split on . ! ? in order; every checkable-fact sentence indexed from F1) ===
F1: This guide explains how to prepare an ordinary household to manage safely and comfortably through a power outage lasting up to seventy-two hours. [72-hour scope]
F2: It is written for a temperate climate and assumes no backup generator. [assumptions]
F3: Work through the sections in order; the earlier sections cover the preparations that matter most. [ordering instruction]
F4: Store four litres of water per person per day, which means twelve litres per person for the full seventy-two hours. [4 L/person/day; 12 L/person/72h]
F5: Half of that allowance is for drinking, and the other half covers cooking and basic hygiene. [drinking vs cooking/hygiene split]
F6: Store the water in food-grade containers, keep the containers out of direct sunlight, and replace stored water every six months. [food-grade; out of sunlight; 6-month rotation]
F7: If a storm is forecast and you have advance warning, also fill the bathtub; bathtub water is for flushing toilets and washing, never for drinking. [advance-warning bathtub fill; never drink]
F8: Keep a dedicated shelf of non-perishable food sufficient for three days. [3-day shelf]
F9: Good choices are canned beans, canned fish, peanut butter, crackers, dried fruit, and shelf-stable milk. [six named foods]
F10: Choose foods that require no cooking, and make sure you own a manual can opener, because an electric one will be useless. [no-cook; manual can opener]
F11: Eat the perishable food from the refrigerator first, then the food from the freezer, and only then open the emergency shelf. [fridge->freezer->shelf eat order]
F12: An unopened refrigerator keeps food safe for about four hours. [~4 h fridge]
F13: A full freezer holds a safe temperature for about forty-eight hours, but a half-full freezer holds it for only about twenty-four hours, so it is worth keeping the freezer consolidated and full, even if some of the space is taken up by containers of frozen water. [~48h full/~24h half; consolidate; frozen water]
F14: Keep both doors closed as much as possible and tape a note to each door as a reminder. [doors closed; taped notes]
F15: Any perishable food that has spent more than two hours above four degrees Celsius should be discarded; when in doubt, throw it out. [2h/4C discard; when-in-doubt]
F16: Use battery-powered LED lanterns or headlamps as your primary light source, and avoid candles entirely, since candles are a leading cause of house fires during outages. [LED primary; avoid candles/fire cause]
F17: Store at least one light per person, plus two spare sets of batteries for each light. [one light/person; two spare battery sets/light]
F18: Keep one headlamp beside each bed and one lantern in the kitchen, and place them where they can be found in the dark. [headlamp/bed; kitchen lantern; findable in dark]
F19: Never run a camping stove, barbecue, or generator indoors, in a garage, or near a window, because each of these produces carbon monoxide, which is odourless and can be fatal. [CO triple prohibition; odourless/fatal]
F20: Install at least one battery-powered carbon monoxide alarm on each floor where people sleep. [>=1 CO alarm per sleeping floor]
F21: In cold weather, choose one small room on a south-facing side of the home, close its door, and gather everyone there; layered clothing and blankets are safer than any improvised heater. [small south room; layers beat heaters]
F22: If the indoor temperature falls below ten degrees Celsius and you cannot maintain warmth, relocate to a warming centre or a neighbour's home. [10C relocation threshold]
F23: Keep a battery-powered or hand-crank radio for emergency broadcasts, and know the frequency of your local emergency station before the outage begins. [radio; pre-learned frequency]
F24: Charge every phone and power bank whenever a storm is forecast; a full modern power bank of twenty thousand milliamp-hours will recharge a typical phone roughly four times. [charge on forecast; 20,000 mAh ~4 charges]
F25: Put your phone in low-power mode at the start of the outage, and agree in advance on one out-of-area contact whom every member of the household will text, because text messages get through congested networks more reliably than voice calls. [low-power mode; one out-of-area contact; texts beat calls]
F26: Keep a first-aid kit and a seven-day supply of every prescription medication, and rotate the medications so they stay in date. [first-aid kit; 7-day prescriptions; rotate]
F27: Refrigerated medication such as insulin generally remains usable at room temperature for up to twenty-eight days, but confirm the rule for your specific medication with a pharmacist before an emergency happens. [insulin 28 days room temp; pharmacist confirm]
F28: Write down the phone numbers of your pharmacy, your doctor, and the poison-control line on paper, because a dead phone is not a phone book. [paper numbers: pharmacy/doctor/poison-control]
F29: Wait five minutes before switching major appliances back on, so the grid can stabilise. [5-min wait; grid stabilise]
F30: Check the freezer: if food still contains ice crystals, it can be safely refrozen. [ice-crystal refreeze test]
F31: Restock everything used during the outage within one week, starting with water and batteries. [1-week restock; water/batteries first]
F32: Finally, write a one-page note about what was missing or awkward, and fix at least one of those gaps before the next outage arrives. [one-page after-action note; fix one gap]
Total: 32 facts F1..F32 (matches frontier.best.evidence 'F1..F32').

=== STEP 2: CANONICAL SERIALIZATION + HASH ===
The proposal artifact was written verbatim to proposal_raw.json, then re-serialized as canonical JSON (recursive sorted keys, no whitespace) via a node script (JSON.parse -> recursive sorted-key stringify) into proposal_canon.json (6536 bytes). Those exact bytes are the hash preimage and persist on disk.
Exact hash command (run in the p1-r4-compose-line-edit dir):
    sha256sum proposal_canon.json
Digest:
    ad6680fa3478b2fbc4737a2024d4f6f0554fc2b230240ccc8f62cc705e5d16be *proposal_canon.json
seedHex = ad6680fa3478b2fbc4737a2024d4f6f0554fc2b230240ccc8f62cc705e5d16be
Verification: sha256sum proposal_canon.json == seedHex (holds; any third party can re-run).

=== STEP 3: DRAW RULE (8 distinct facts, without replacement) ===
Digest bytes left to right as unsigned integers: b0=0xad=173, b1=0x66=102, b2=0x80=128, b3=0xfa=250, b4=0x34=52, b5=0x78=120, b6=0xb2=178, b7=0xfb=251, ...
Remaining list starts [F1..F32] (32 items), 0-indexed by position. For each draw k: idx = b_k mod (remaining count); pick remaining[idx]; remove it.
Draw 0: count=32, 173 mod 32 = 13 -> position 13 -> F14. Remove F14.
Draw 1: count=31, 102 mod 31 = 9  -> position 9  -> F10. Remove F10.
Draw 2: count=30, 128 mod 30 = 8  -> position 8  -> F9.  Remove F9.
Draw 3: count=29, 250 mod 29 = 18 -> position 18 -> F22. Remove F22.
Draw 4: count=28, 52 mod 28  = 24 -> position 24 -> F29. Remove F29.
Draw 5: count=27, 120 mod 27 = 12 -> position 12 -> F16. Remove F16.
Draw 6: count=26, 178 mod 26 = 22 -> position 22 -> F28. Remove F28.
Draw 7: count=25, 251 mod 25 = 1  -> position 1  -> F2.  Remove F2.
Drawn (order): F14, F10, F9, F22, F29, F16, F28, F2 (8 distinct).

=== STEP 4: COMPREHENSION QUESTIONS (expected answers quoted from the ORIGINAL sentence) ===
Q1 (F14): In the refrigerator/freezer section, what must you do with both appliance doors, and what reminder step goes with it? => "Keep both doors closed as much as possible and tape a note to each door as a reminder."
Q2 (F10): What kind of can opener must you make sure to own for the emergency food, and why? => "make sure you own a manual can opener, because an electric one will be useless"
Q3 (F9): Name the recommended non-perishable food choices for the emergency shelf. => "canned beans, canned fish, peanut butter, crackers, dried fruit, and shelf-stable milk"
Q4 (F22): At what indoor temperature, and under what condition, should you relocate, and to where? => "If the indoor temperature falls below ten degrees Celsius and you cannot maintain warmth, relocate to a warming centre or a neighbour's home."
Q5 (F29): When power returns, how long should you wait before switching major appliances back on, and why? => "Wait five minutes before switching major appliances back on, so the grid can stabilise."
Q6 (F16): What primary light source is recommended, and what light source should be avoided and why? => "Use battery-powered LED lanterns or headlamps as your primary light source, and avoid candles entirely, since candles are a leading cause of house fires during outages."
Q7 (F28): Which three phone numbers should you write down on paper, and why? => "Write down the phone numbers of your pharmacy, your doctor, and the poison-control line on paper, because a dead phone is not a phone book."
Q8 (F2): What climate is this guide written for, and what backup equipment does it assume you do not have? => "It is written for a temperate climate and assumes no backup generator."

Gap seat did not judge the proposal's merits (separation, not evaluation). proposal_canon.json left in place for the auditor.
